label: Havana
sub: Cuba's capital and largest city. Center of revolutionary government since 1959 and site of the US Embassy reopening in 2015. Population ~2.1 million.

label: Playa Girón (Bay of Pigs)
sub: Main landing beach of the April 1961 CIA-sponsored exile invasion. Site of Cuba's decisive victory over Brigade 2506 in 72 hours. Now hosts a museum to the battle.

label: Playa Larga
sub: Northern landing site of the Bay of Pigs invasion on April 17, 1961. Brigade 2506 paratroopers landed here to cut off the Ciénaga de Zapata. Cuban forces repulsed the landing within hours.

label: Bay of Pigs (Bahía de Cochinos)
sub: The bay itself where the 1961 CIA-sponsored invasion fleet assembled. Cuban militia and Revolutionary Armed Forces defeated the invasion force here, earning a major propaganda victory for Castro.

label: Santiago de Cuba
sub: Cuba's second largest city and the cradle of the Revolution. Site of the Moncada Barracks attack (1953), where Castro's revolutionary career began. Fidel Castro is buried at the Santa Ifigenia Cemetery here.

label: Moncada Barracks
sub: Site of Fidel Castro's failed July 26, 1953 assault that launched the Cuban Revolution. The barracks walls still bear bullet holes (replicated for historical preservation). Now a school and museum.

label: Guantánamo Bay (US Naval Base)
sub: US naval base leased since 1903; Cuba has refused to cash US rent checks since 1959 and demands its return. Used as detention facility for migrants (1994 Balseros) and post-9/11 terrorism suspects.

label: Santa Clara
sub: Site of the decisive December 1958 battle of the Cuban Revolution. Che Guevara's column captured the armored train and the city, breaking Batista's defenses. Che's mausoleum and memorial are located here.

label: Sierra Maestra
sub: Mountain range in southeastern Cuba that served as Fidel Castro's guerrilla base from 1956–1959. Radio Rebelde broadcast from here. Now a national park and revolutionary heritage site.

label: San Cristóbal Missile Site
sub: Primary site of Soviet medium-range ballistic missiles (MRBMs) discovered by US U-2 reconnaissance on October 14, 1962, triggering the Cuban Missile Crisis. Four launch complexes with R-12 missiles were constructed here.

label: Sagua la Grande — IRBM Sites
sub: Site of Soviet intermediate-range ballistic missile (IRBM/R-14) construction during the 1962 Missile Crisis. The IRBMs had a 2,200-mile range, capable of hitting most of the continental US. Never became operational before withdrawal.

label: Remedios Missile Zone
sub: Area in central Cuba containing additional Soviet missile infrastructure and support bases during the 1962 crisis. Soviet surface-to-air missile (SAM) batteries were operational here and shot down Major Anderson's U-2.

label: Cienfuegos
sub: Major port city and industrial center. Site of Cuba's only nuclear power plant project (Juraguá Nuclear Power Plant), abandoned 1992 due to US pressure and Soviet collapse. The uncompleted plant remains a monument to Cuba's developmental ambitions.

label: Matanzas
sub: Major city east of Havana, scene of significant July 11, 2021 protests. The city's Supertanker Base suffered a massive explosion and fire in August 2022, destroying fuel reserves and worsening the energy crisis.

label: Port of Mariel
sub: Port from which 125,000 Cubans sailed to Florida during the 1980 Mariel Boatlift. Now site of the Special Development Zone of Mariel (ZEDM), Cuba's flagship foreign investment zone established in 2013.

label: US Embassy Havana
sub: Reopened July 20, 2015 after 54 years as the US Interests Section. Site of Havana Syndrome health incidents reported by US diplomats beginning in 2016–2017. One of the most watched diplomatic buildings in the Western Hemisphere.

label: Isle of Youth (Isla de la Juventud)
sub: Formerly Isle of Pines; site of the Model Prison (Presidio Modelo) where Fidel Castro was imprisoned 1953–1955 after the Moncada attack. Now holds one of Cuba's top international secondary schools for African students, reflecting Cuba's solidarity policy.

label: Trinidad
sub: UNESCO World Heritage colonial city and major tourism hub. One of Cuba's most visited sites, showcasing the tourism-centered economic model Cuba turned to after Soviet collapse. Now affected by fuel shortages limiting tourist transportation.

label: Varadero Beach Resort
sub: Cuba's premier international beach resort. Since the 1990s, the government developed Varadero as a hard-currency tourism enclave separated from ordinary Cubans (tourist apartheid). A symbol of Cuba's two-tier economy under the embargo era.

label: Camagüey
sub: Cuba's third-largest city; major site of July 11, 2021 protests. Demonstrators clashed with security forces. Camagüey has a strong Catholic and dissident tradition and sent a disproportionate share of political prisoners after the 2021 crackdown.

label: Holguín
sub: Major eastern city that saw protests during July 11, 2021 and subsequent economic demonstrations. Near Birán, birthplace of both Fidel and Raúl Castro. Cuba's nickel mining region centered here; nickel is a key export.

label: Pinar del Río
sub: Cuba's westernmost province and tobacco capital (Vuelta Abajo); produces the world's finest tobacco for Cohiba and Montecristo cigars, heavily affected by US embargo on Cuban goods. Suffered severe tornado damage in 2019.

label: Ciudad Libertad (former Camp Columbia)
sub: Cuba's largest pre-revolution military base; Batista fled from here on Jan 1, 1959. Castro spoke here on January 8, 1959. Transformed by the revolution into a school city (Ciudad Libertad) as a symbol of converting military to educational use.

label: La Cabaña Fortress
sub: 18th-century fortress across Havana harbor; became Cuba's largest prison after 1959. Che Guevara oversaw revolutionary tribunals here 1959–1960, executing hundreds of former Batista officials. Now a museum and tourist site.

label: Playa Las Coloradas (Granma Landing Site)
sub: Exact beach where Fidel Castro and 81 revolutionaries landed from the yacht Granma on December 2, 1956, starting the guerrilla campaign. A replica of the Granma yacht is on display. National monument.

label: Havana — Centro Habana
sub: Centro Habana and Parque Central were epicenters of the July 11, 2021 protests in the capital. Security forces fired rubber bullets and made mass arrests. Protesters also marched along the Malecón, Cuba's iconic sea-front promenade.

label: Birán — Castro Family Estate
sub: Rural estate in Holguín province where Fidel and Raúl Castro were born and raised. Their father, Ángel Castro, was a wealthy sugarcane farmer. The property was later nationalized and turned into a museum to the Castros' origins.

label: Bay of Pigs Museum
sub: Museum at Playa Girón commemorating Cuba's victory in the Bay of Pigs invasion, April 17–19, 1961. Displays captured Brigade 2506 equipment, including a Sea Fury fighter. Central to Cuba's nationalist narrative of defeating the 'imperialist invasion.'

label: Artemisa
sub: Western province town that was a major protest site on July 11, 2021. Demonstrators here faced heavy police and paramilitary response. The province saw some of the harshest sentencing of protesters in subsequent trials.

label: Sancti Spíritus
sub: Central Cuba city and agricultural center heavily impacted by Special Period food scarcity (1991–2000). Sugar mills that once defined Cuba's economy were shuttered during the 2002 sugar industry rationalization when half of Cuba's 156 mills were closed.

label: Batabanó Port
sub: Southern port used as a staging point by Brigade 2506 supply vessels during the Bay of Pigs invasion. Cuban coastal defense forces interdicted Brigade supply ships here, cutting off the invasion force from reinforcement and dooming the operation.

label: Guantánamo City
sub: City adjacent to but separate from the US naval base. Cuban civilians who worked at the base formed the 'fence community' until Cuba ended civilian employment at the base in 1961. The city has remained Cuba's easternmost major urban center.
